DISCOVERY, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE JUL-24-2020 HEARING RE: DEFENDANT SHANKAR'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS AND ELECTRONICALLY STORED INFORMATION (SET ONE); AND FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS AGAINST COLLECT ACCESS LLC, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT'S MOTION TO COMPEL DOCUMENTS IS GRANTED. PLAINTIFF SHALL PROVIDE FULL AND COMPLETE RESPONSES IN COMPLIANCE WITH CCP 2031.230 TO REQUEST NO. 3 AND SHALL PRODUCE DOCUMENTS WITHIN IT POSSESSION, CUSTODY OR CONTROL, INCLUDING EIS WITHIN 20-DAYS OF THE ORDER. WHERE REDACTION IS DEEMED NECESSARY AND APPROPRIATE, SUFFICIENT INFORMATION SHALL BE PROVIDED TO ALLOW THE OTHER PARTY TO EVALUATE THE CLAIM OF PRIVILEGE OR PROVIDE A PRIVILEGE LOG. CCP 2031.240(C)(1). DEFENSE COUNSEL TO SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER TO THE JUDGE PRO TEM. JUDGE PRO TEM: STEPHANIE KRMPOTIC; CLERK: SEAN KANE; NOT REPORTED. (302/JPT)

Re-Notice Of Defendant Shankars Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents And Electronically Stored Information (Set One); And For Monetary Sanctions Against Collect Access Llc
Off Calendar Jul-24-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Atty. CHI N. DINH, Esq. of Law Office of Chi N. Dinh (Tel: 510-512-6606) for Defendant
Vishnu Shankar;
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing Re: Defendant Shankar's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents And Electronically Stored Information (Set One); And For Monetary Sanctions Against Collect Access LLC.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant's motion to compel documents is granted. Plaintiff shall provide full and complete responses in compliance with CCP 2031.230 to Request No. 3 and shall produce documents within it possession, custody or control, including EIS within 20-days of the order. Where redaction is deemed necessary and appropriate, sufficient information shall be provided to allow the other party to evaluate the claim of privilege or provide a privilege log. CCP 2031.240(c)(1).

Defense counsel to submit a proposed order to the judge pro tem.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternate For Summary Adjudication Against Defendant Vishnu Shankar
Off Calendar Jun-22-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF COLLECT ACCES LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternate For Summary Adjudication Against Defendant Vishnu Shankar.

No appearances made by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Plaintiff Collect Access, LLC.'s motion for summary judgment or alternatively summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. Plaintiff fails to maintain its initial burden of production. First, the Equipment Finance Agreement (par. 5) and Guarantee expressly state that Washington law governs the parties' relationship. While the agreements give the "secured party" the option of commencing a collection action in a non-Washington court, they do not provide that non-Washington law will apply and plaintiff provides no choice of law analysis regarding why the court should apply California law (or Nevada law, which is also cited in plaintiff's reply) in this case. Second, the amount of damages is unclear. Plaintiff asserts that defendant entered into a 60-month finance agreement at $1,791.48 per month. The finance agreement also includes a $350 administrative fee. The court and defendant calculate the total contract sum to be $107,838.80. Plaintiff, however, claims that the total was $110,589.04. (Plaintiff's UMF 10.) To compound the confusion, plaintiff claims that on 6/6/17 defendant owed $91,528.83 and on 10/25/17 the sum was $79,697.00. (Essa Dec., pars. 11, 14; Plaintiff's UMFs 14, 18, 22.) There is no cogent explanation regarding how plaintiff arrived at these numbers. Lastly, defendant presents evidence that the secured party sold the equipment for $6,650. (Shankar Dec., par. 19, Ex. A.) The secured creditor has the burden of proving that the resale of collateral was accomplished in a commercially reasonable manner. (Timms v. James (1980) 28 Wash.App. 76, 79 [remanding to determine whether sale of dental equipment was carried out in a commercially reasonable manner where plaintiff did not produce any evidence on subject at trial].) Given the vast disparity between the original contract price and the repossession sale price, defendant has rebutted any presumption that the sale was commercially reasonable. (See RCW 62A.9A-610 [disposition of collateral after default]; Swanson v. May, 40 Wash.App.148, 155 (1985) [factors include the relationship of the price obtained to the recognized market price].) Defendant cannot waive the commercial reasonableness requirement. (See Security State Bank v. Burk, 100 Wash.App. 94, 100 (2000) (citing repealed RCW 62A.9-501(3)(b) and RCW 62A.9-504(3)); Atchley v. Pepperidge Farm, Inc. 2008 WL 5377714 (E.D.Wash. Dec. 22, 2008) [citing Security State and explaining new iteration of the UCC].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)
